questions.stem: Epidemiology is best defined as the study of:

options.B: The distribution and determinants of disease frequency in populations, applied to disease control

questions.explanation: Epidemiology studies distribution & determinants of disease frequency in populations and applies this to control health problems.

questions.stem: Who is regarded as the father of demography, having created life tables from London death records?
questions.explanation: John Graunt — analysed parish death records and built the first life tables.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a measure of disease FREQUENCY (not association)?
questions.explanation: Prevalence is a frequency measure; the others are measures of association.

questions.stem: Prevalence depends on incidence and:
questions.explanation: Prevalence ≈ incidence × duration.

questions.stem: A dynamic (open) population is characterized by:

options.A: Permanent membership defined by an event
options.B: Transient membership defined by being in a state/condition

questions.explanation: A dynamic/open population has transient membership (e.g. residents of a city).

questions.stem: In a town of 50,000 residents, 1,500 people currently have type 2 diabetes on 1 July. What is the point prevalence of diabetes?
questions.explanation: Prevalence = existing cases ÷ total population. Worked solution: Prevalence = 1,500 / 50,000 = 0.03 = 3%

questions.stem: Which study design is most efficient for studying a RARE disease?
questions.explanation: Case-control starts from cases → efficient for rare diseases.

questions.stem: In a cohort study, subjects are grouped by their:

options.A: Disease status, then exposure is traced backward
options.B: Exposure status, then followed forward for the outcome

questions.explanation: Cohorts sample on exposure and follow forward to the outcome.

questions.stem: The measure of association typically derived from a cohort study is:
questions.explanation: Cohorts yield relative risk (risk or rate ratio).

questions.stem: An ambidirectional (bidirectional) study contains:
questions.explanation: Ambidirectional cohorts have both prospective and retrospective components.

questions.stem: 1,200 people are screened; 200 already have the disease and are excluded. The remaining are followed for 4 years, during which 150 develop the disease (no losses). What is the 4-year cumulative incidence?
questions.explanation: Cumulative incidence = new cases ÷ population AT RISK at start (exclude prevalent cases). Worked solution: Population at risk = 1,200 − 200 = 1,000 CI = 150 / 1,000 = 0.15 = 15% over 4 years

questions.stem: The three components of the epidemiologic triangle are:
questions.explanation: Host · Agent (pathogen) · Environment.

questions.stem: John Snow is credited with demonstrating that:

options.A: Scurvy is caused by vitamin deficiency
options.B: Cholera is transmitted by faecally-contaminated water
options.C: Smallpox can be prevented by vaccination

questions.explanation: Snow linked cholera to contaminated drinking water (Broad St pump).

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a Bradford Hill criterion for causation?
questions.explanation: Randomization is a design method, not a Hill criterion.

questions.stem: The Bradford Hill criterion of 'temporality' means that:

options.B: The cause must precede the effect
options.C: The dose relates to the response

questions.explanation: Temporality: the exposure (cause) must come before the outcome.

questions.stem: A study accumulates 2,500 person-years of follow-up, during which 50 new cases of disease occur. What is the incidence rate?
questions.explanation: Incidence rate = new cases ÷ total person-time. Worked solution: IR = 50 / 2,500 = 0.02 per person-year = 20 per 1,000 person-years

questions.stem: A confounder must satisfy all of the following EXCEPT:

options.A: It is associated with the exposure
options.B: It is an independent risk factor for the disease
options.C: It lies on the causal pathway between exposure and disease
options.D: It is not an effect of the exposure

questions.explanation: A confounder must NOT be an intermediate on the causal pathway.

questions.stem: Randomization in a trial is most valuable because it:

options.B: Balances both known AND unknown confounders (with large n)
options.D: Removes the need for a control group

questions.explanation: Randomization balances confounders you can't even measure.

questions.stem: The main bacterial indicator organism used to assess water quality is:
questions.explanation: E. coli (faecal coliform) is the principal indicator of faecal contamination.

questions.stem: At a banquet, 250 people ate the buffet and 75 developed gastroenteritis within 12 hours. What is the attack rate?
questions.explanation: Attack rate = new cases ÷ population at risk during the outbreak. Worked solution: Attack rate = 75 / 250 = 0.30 = 30%

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a method of disinfecting drinking water?
questions.explanation: Aluminium sulfate is a coagulant (clarifies water) — not a disinfectant.

questions.stem: Cryptosporidium, responsible for the 1993 Milwaukee outbreak, is a:
questions.explanation: Cryptosporidium is a waterborne protozoan parasite (a classic trick — not a bacterium).

questions.stem: Ground-level ozone (O₃) is classified as a:
questions.explanation: O₃ forms by atmospheric reactions → secondary pollutant.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a component of clean, unpolluted air?
questions.explanation: Ammonia is not a component of clean air.

questions.stem: In a cohort, 90 of 300 smokers develop disease and 30 of 300 non-smokers develop disease. What is the risk ratio for smokers?
questions.explanation: Risk ratio = risk in exposed ÷ risk in unexposed. Worked solution: Risk (smokers) = 90/300 = 0.30 Risk (non-smokers) = 30/300 = 0.10 RR = 0.30 / 0.10 = 3.0

questions.stem: The primary anthropogenic greenhouse gas is:
questions.explanation: CO₂ is the dominant greenhouse gas.

questions.stem: Foodborne INTOXICATION (as opposed to infection) is characterized by:

options.A: Multiplication of the microbe in the intestine
options.B: A pre-formed toxin present in the food

questions.explanation: Intoxication is caused by a pre-formed toxin in the food (no intestinal multiplication needed).

questions.stem: Aflatoxins, the most widespread mycotoxins, are produced by Aspergillus and found mainly in:
questions.explanation: Aflatoxins contaminate cereal grains & nuts.

questions.stem: According to WHO, the safe occupational noise limit for an 8-hour shift is approximately:
questions.explanation: WHO safe threshold ≈ < 75 dB for an 8-h shift.

questions.stem: The Nuremberg Code is best known for:

options.B: Ten principles emphasizing voluntary informed consent

questions.explanation: The Nuremberg Code = 10 principles stressing voluntary informed consent.

questions.stem: Which measure counts only NEW cases of disease?
questions.explanation: Incidence measures new cases; prevalence measures existing cases.

questions.stem: The denominator for an incidence measure includes only:
questions.explanation: Incidence uses the population at risk (excludes existing cases & immunes).

questions.stem: Person-time is the denominator used in:
questions.explanation: Incidence rate uses person-time (time intrinsic to the denominator).

questions.stem: In a case-control study of lung cancer and asbestos: among cases, 80 were exposed and 20 unexposed; among controls, 40 were exposed and 60 unexposed. What is the odds ratio?
questions.explanation: OR = cross-product ratio (ad/bc). Worked solution: OR = (80 × 60) / (20 × 40) = 4,800 / 800 = 6.0

questions.stem: The odds ratio is the characteristic measure of association derived from a:
questions.explanation: Case-control studies yield the odds ratio.

questions.stem: In a double-blind randomized controlled trial:

options.A: Only the patient knows the allocation
options.B: Only the researcher knows the allocation
options.C: Neither the researcher nor the patient knows the allocation

questions.explanation: Neither the researcher nor the patient knows the treatment allocation.

questions.stem: 'Equipoise', required for an ethical trial, refers to:

options.B: Genuine uncertainty about which treatment is superior

questions.explanation: Equipoise = genuine uncertainty about which treatment is better.

questions.stem: Which ethics document consists of ten principles formulated after WWII?
questions.explanation: The Nuremberg Code — 10 principles.

questions.stem: The Tuskegee Syphilis Study is chiefly remembered as an example of:

options.B: Research that was legal at the time but deeply unethical

questions.explanation: Tuskegee — legal at the time but unethical (no informed consent, treatment withheld).

questions.stem: In a cohort, 40 of 200 exposed individuals develop disease, and 20 of 200 unexposed develop disease. What is the risk difference?
questions.explanation: Risk difference = risk(exposed) − risk(unexposed). Worked solution: Risk (exposed) = 40/200 = 0.20 Risk (unexposed) = 20/200 = 0.10 RD = 0.20 − 0.10 = 0.10

questions.stem: When two factors together produce an effect GREATER than the sum of their individual effects, this is called:
questions.explanation: A supra-additive combined effect = synergy.

questions.stem: Standardization is used to remove the ___ effect of variables such as age so populations can be compared:
questions.explanation: Standardization removes the confounding effect (usually of age).

questions.stem: Direct standardization requires knowledge of:

options.B: The age-specific rates of the study population

questions.explanation: Direct standardization uses the study population's age-specific rates applied to a standard population.

questions.stem: The Standardized Mortality Ratio (SMR) is calculated as:
questions.explanation: SMR = observed ÷ expected deaths (×100).

questions.stem: In an occupational cohort, 60 deaths were observed. Applying national age-specific rates, 40 deaths were expected. What is the SMR?
questions.explanation: SMR = observed ÷ expected × 100. An SMR of 150 means 50% excess mortality. Worked solution: SMR = (60 / 40) × 100 = 150 → 50% higher mortality than the reference population

questions.stem: Meta-analysis is best defined as:

options.B: The quantitative (statistical) synthesis of results from individual studies
options.C: Analysis of a single large trial

questions.explanation: Meta-analysis = quantitative synthesis of individual study results.

questions.stem: Statistical power is the ability of a study to:

options.B: Detect an association if one truly exists

questions.explanation: Power = probability of detecting a true association.

questions.stem: Selecting controls by random-digit dialling of telephone subscribers identifies which type of controls?
questions.explanation: Random-digit dialling samples the general public → population controls.

questions.stem: During an outbreak, 500 people became infected and 25 of them died. What is the case-fatality rate?
questions.explanation: Case-fatality rate = deaths from the disease ÷ people with the disease. Worked solution: CFR = 25 / 500 = 0.05 = 5%

questions.stem: The 'healthy worker effect' describes the observation that:

options.A: Workers are exposed to more hazards
options.B: Employed populations have lower morbidity/mortality than the general population
options.C: Workers refuse to participate in studies

questions.explanation: Workers are healthier than the general public → biases comparisons using the general population.

questions.stem: Asbestos primarily damages health via:
questions.explanation: Inhaled fibres harm the respiratory tract (asbestosis, mesothelioma).

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a recognized type of environmental pollution?
questions.explanation: Technology is not a pollution type (water, air, soil, thermal, noise, light are).

questions.stem: Thermal inversion is:

options.B: A meteorological phenomenon that traps pollutants near the ground

questions.explanation: A warm air layer traps cooler air below → pollutants accumulate.

questions.stem: A clinic has 2,000 registered patients. On a given day, 300 have hypertension, 100 have diabetes, and 40 have both. What is the prevalence of hypertension?
questions.explanation: Prevalence of hypertension = people with hypertension ÷ total population. (Diabetes/overlap are distractors.) Worked solution: Prevalence = 300 / 2,000 = 0.15 = 15%

questions.stem: Compared with WHO air-quality guidelines, EU limit values are generally:
questions.explanation: WHO guidelines are purely health-based & stricter; EU limits are less strict (balance feasibility/economics).

questions.stem: The Mediterranean diet pyramid recommends:

options.B: Limited meat, roughly 200–400 g per week

questions.explanation: Med diet = limited meat (~200–400 g/week).

questions.stem: Which foodborne agent is notable for a high case-fatality (5–10%)?
questions.explanation: Botulism (C. botulinum toxin) has high case-fatality.

questions.stem: Bacterial dysentery (blood and mucus in the stool) is classically caused by:
questions.explanation: Shigella → bacillary dysentery.

questions.stem: How is climate change most likely to affect malaria?

options.B: Through temperature and rainfall changes favouring mosquito breeding

questions.explanation: Warming + altered rainfall favour mosquito breeding and shift malaria's range.

questions.stem: BMI classification cut-offs (e.g. 18.5–25 as normal):

options.B: Are not valid for all ages (children/elderly differ)

questions.explanation: BMI cut-offs are not valid at all ages.

questions.stem: Descriptive epidemiology characterizes disease according to:
questions.explanation: Descriptive epi = person, place, time.

questions.stem: A cross-sectional study is best described as:

options.B: A 'snapshot' measuring exposure and disease at a single point in time

questions.explanation: Cross-sectional = a snapshot (good for prevalence).

questions.stem: An ecological study uses which unit of analysis?
questions.explanation: Ecological studies analyse the population/group, not the individual.

questions.stem: The 'ecological fallacy' is the error of:

options.B: Inferring individual-level relationships from group-level data

questions.explanation: Ecological fallacy = inferring individual associations from group data.

questions.stem: 200 women free of breast cancer are followed for 10 years with no losses; 16 develop breast cancer. What is the 10-year cumulative incidence?
questions.explanation: Cumulative incidence = new cases ÷ population at risk at start. Worked solution: CI = 16 / 200 = 0.08 = 8% over 10 years

questions.stem: Cumulative incidence assumes:

options.B: No loss to follow-up (a closed cohort)
options.D: That time is in the denominator

questions.explanation: CI assumes complete follow-up (no losses) → closed cohort.

questions.stem: When there is NO association between exposure and disease, the risk ratio, odds ratio, and risk difference respectively equal:
questions.explanation: Ratios (RR, OR) = 1; risk difference = 0 → 1, 1, 0.

questions.stem: A risk ratio of 0.25 indicates that the exposure is:
questions.explanation: RR < 1 → protective (here a 75% risk reduction).

questions.stem: Among 1,000 vaccinated people, 20 get influenza; among 1,000 unvaccinated, 80 get influenza. What is the risk ratio (vaccinated vs unvaccinated)?
questions.explanation: RR = risk(vaccinated) ÷ risk(unvaccinated); RR<1 means the vaccine is protective. Worked solution: Risk (vaccinated) = 20/1,000 = 0.02 Risk (unvaccinated) = 80/1,000 = 0.08 RR = 0.02 / 0.08 = 0.25 (75% lower risk)

questions.stem: Matching in a case-control study:

options.B: Controls confounding and increases study power
options.D: Is only used in cohort studies

questions.explanation: Matching controls confounding & improves power/efficiency.

questions.stem: Hospital controls in a case-control study should ideally:

options.A: Come from a different region than the cases
options.B: Come from the same catchment area as the cases

questions.explanation: Hospital controls should share the cases' catchment area.

questions.stem: A case-control study finds: cases = 45 exposed, 15 unexposed; controls = 30 exposed, 90 unexposed. What is the odds ratio?
James Lind is credited with conducting:

options.B: The first recorded controlled clinical trial (scurvy)

questions.explanation: James Lind — first recorded controlled clinical trial (scurvy, 1747).

questions.stem: The thalidomide disaster mainly highlighted the need for:
questions.explanation: Thalidomide → the need for rigorous drug-safety testing, especially in pregnancy.

questions.stem: The three principles of the Belmont Report are:
questions.explanation: Respect for autonomy, beneficence, justice.

questions.stem: During a foodborne outbreak, 90 of 120 people who ate the chicken became ill. What is the attack rate among those who ate the chicken?
questions.explanation: Food-specific attack rate = ill among those who ate the item ÷ total who ate the item. Worked solution: Attack rate = 90 / 120 = 0.75 = 75%

questions.stem: Current drinking-water legislation permits a maximum of:
questions.explanation: 0 enterococci / 100 mL.

questions.stem: Chlorine is typically added during which step of water treatment?
questions.explanation: Chlorine is the final disinfection step, leaving a protective residual.

questions.stem: Bacterial indicators (e.g. E. coli) in water signify that:

options.B: The water has mixed with intestinal (faecal) contents

questions.explanation: They indicate faecal contamination.

questions.stem: A cohort study accumulates 5,000 person-years of follow-up and observes 25 new cases of stroke. What is the incidence rate?
questions.explanation: Incidence rate = new cases ÷ total person-time. Worked solution: IR = 25 / 5,000 = 0.005 per person-year = 5 per 1,000 person-years

questions.stem: The most frequent cause of foodborne epidemics is:
questions.explanation: Salmonella causes the most foodborne outbreaks.

questions.stem: A prerequisite for foodborne INFECTION includes all of the following EXCEPT:

options.A: The microorganism is present in the food
options.B: The microorganism multiplies in the food to a high concentration
options.C: The microorganism multiplies in the intestinal tract

questions.explanation: Multiplying in the food defines intoxication, not infection.

questions.stem: Haemolytic-uraemic syndrome (bloody diarrhoea + acute kidney injury) is caused by:
questions.explanation: E. coli O157:H7 (Shiga toxin) → HUS.

questions.stem: Respirable particles (PM10) do NOT cause:
questions.explanation: PM10 harms heart/lungs and raises mortality — not skin.

questions.stem: Stratospheric ozone is destroyed mainly by:
questions.explanation: CFCs release chlorine radicals that destroy stratospheric ozone.

questions.stem: Which energy source is non-renewable?
questions.explanation: Nuclear depends on finite uranium.

questions.stem: Minamata disease (Japan, 1953) was caused by:
questions.explanation: Mercury (methylmercury) poisoning.

questions.stem: Emerging/re-emerging pathogens include all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: S. pneumoniae is long-established, not emerging.

questions.stem: 'Genomic surveillance' in public health refers to:

options.B: Sequencing pathogen genomes to track spread and variants

questions.explanation: Sequencing pathogen genomes to monitor transmission and variants.

questions.stem: Occupational RISK is best defined as:

options.A: Anything with the potential to cause harm
options.B: The likelihood and severity of injury/disease from exposure to a hazard
options.C: Any physical agent in the workplace

questions.explanation: Risk = likelihood AND severity of harm from a hazard. ('Anything with potential to cause harm' defines a hazard.)
